verses.commentary: The Table of Nations begins. This chapter maps humanity's spread across the earth from Noah's three sons. The origin of the nations known to ancient Israel.
Japheth's line is listed first. These descendants settle in regions to the north and west: Asia Minor, Greece, and beyond. The gentile nations of the Mediterranean world.
Further subdivisions. The genealogy creates a family tree of nations.
Javan represents the Greeks (Ionia). These names appear throughout the Old Testament as distant maritime peoples.
The diversification of languages is mentioned before Babel (chapter 11). The nations spread geographically and linguistically. God's command to fill the earth (9:1) is fulfilled.
Ham's line includes the nations to the south: Africa and the Near East. Egypt (Mitsrayim) and Canaan are particularly significant for Israel's story.
Cush generally refers to the region of modern Sudan/Ethiopia. These are African and Arabian peoples.
Nimrod stands out. He is the first named individual in the chapter. A mighty warrior (gibbor, גִּבּוֹר) can mean hero or tyrant. The ambiguity is intentional.
Nimrod becomes proverbial. 'Before the LORD' might mean in God's sight or in defiance of God. The phrase is ambiguous. His prowess is legendary but the moral evaluation is unclear.
Nimrod builds cities and a kingdom. Babylon (Babel) will be the site of the tower (chapter 11). Shinar is southern Mesopotamia. Empire building begins.
Nimrod's influence spreads to Assyria in the north. Nineveh becomes the capital of the Assyrian Empire, later a threat to Israel. Human civilization advances.
The great city may refer to Nineveh or to the entire complex of cities. Urbanism and centralized power grow.
Egyptian peoples and territories are listed. Egypt will play a major role in Israel's story (slavery and exodus).
The Philistines are noted. They will be Israel's perennial enemies in the land of Canaan. Their origin is traced to Egypt's line.
Canaan's descendants occupy the land God will promise to Abraham. Sidon represents the Phoenicians to the north. The Hittites are a major ancient power.
These are the peoples living in the land of Canaan. Their names recur in the conquest narratives (Joshua). They are under the curse of 9:25.
More Canaanite peoples. The list is comprehensive, showing the complexity of the land's inhabitants.
The Canaanite peoples spread throughout the region. This sets the stage for Israel's entry into the land.
Geographical boundaries of Canaan. From the north (Sidon) to the south (Gaza and the Dead Sea cities). This is the promised land.
Summary of Ham's line. Clans, languages, territories, nations: the fourfold organization of humanity.
Shem's line comes last, position of emphasis. Eber (עֵבֶר) is the ancestor of the Hebrews (Ivrim, עִבְרִים). This is the line that will carry the promise.
Shem's descendants populate Mesopotamia and beyond. Ashur is Assyria. Arphaxad is in the line leading to Abraham.
Aram is the Arameans (Syria). Job is from Uz. These are the peoples surrounding Israel.
The line narrows. Eber is highlighted again. From him will come the Hebrew people.
Peleg (פֶּלֶג) means division. The name suggests the scattering at Babel (chapter 11) or a geographical split. His time marks a turning point.
Joktan's line represents Arabian tribes. The genealogy spreads wide.
More Arabian peoples. The list shows the breadth of Shem's descendants.
Sheba appears again (also in Ham's line, verse 7). Arabian kingdoms.
Ophir is famous for gold. These are distant, exotic lands from Israel's perspective.
Geographical summary. The eastern regions, toward Arabia.
Summary of Shem's line, parallel to verse 20 (Ham) and verse 5 (Japheth).

Conclusion. All nations trace back to Noah. Unity at the root, diversity in expression. The table shows both God's blessing (be fruitful and multiply) and the coming problem (Tower of Babel). Humanity is one family but will soon scatter in judgment and grace.
